title: Kanelbullar
subtitle: Swedish cinnamon buns with cardamom in the dough
excerpt: Swedish kanelbullar are denser and spicier than their American counterpart, with cardamom kneaded into the dough, a filling of butter, sugar, and cinnamon, and a characteristic twisted knot shape. They bake quickly at high heat to a golden, slightly crisp exterior.
sourceNotes: Kanelbullar are documented in Swedish household cookbooks from the early 20th century and became a national institution after the establishment of Kanelbullens dag (Cinnamon Bun Day) in 1999. The characteristic cardamom in the dough distinguishes them from both American cinnamon rolls and Danish pastry. Pre-1928 Swedish baking texts in the Nordic Museum archives record the cardamom-enriched dough and the knotted shaping method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked buns individually once fully cooled. Warm in a 170°C oven for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prove overnight in the refrigerator after shaping. Bring to room temperature for 45 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 200°C. High heat keeps the exterior crisp while the centre stays soft.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Swedish bun shaping method: a strip of filled dough is wrapped once around two fingers, then the tail is threaded through the loop and tucked under the base. The shape holds the filling inside the folds and creates a crisp exterior on the exposed dough.

content.text: Kanelbullar are not the soft American spiral. They are tighter, spicier, and baked at a higher heat so the outside crisps while the inside stays just tender. Cardamom in the dough is not optional; it is the reason the bun tastes like itself rather than a generic sweet roll.

attrs.prepNote: freshly ground from pods if possible
beaten with a pinch of sugar, for glazing
or crushed brown sugar, for scattering

content.text: Combine the flour, yeast, salt, sugar, and cardamom. Add the warm milk and soft butter and bring together to a dough. Knead for 10 minutes by hand or 6 minutes in a stand mixer with a dough hook, until smooth and elastic. The dough will be soft but not sticky.
Place the dough in an oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth, and leave until doubled. About 60 minutes in a warm kitchen.

Mix the filling ingredients to a smooth paste. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to a rectangle about 40×50 cm. Spread the filling evenly to within 1 cm of the edges. Fold the dough in thirds lengthwise to make a long strip about 13 cm wide, pressing the layers gently together. Cut crosswise into 16 strips of about 2.5 cm each. For the
, wrap each strip once around two fingers, then thread the tail through the loop and tuck it under the base. Alternatively, simply coil each strip into a round on the tray for the simpler version.

Arrange the buns on lined baking trays with 5 cm between each. Cover loosely and leave for 30 minutes until slightly puffed. Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Brush each bun with beaten egg and scatter with pearl sugar. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until deep gold on the peaks and set in the centre. They should feel light and spring back to a gentle press. Cool on a rack; they are best eaten warm, within an hour of baking.
